author: ptrknow
title: Philosophy of Computer
comment_content:
Main Outline of the Content:

Part 0: Preliminaries
Set theory, function, relation, language, countability

Part 1: Automata Theory
1.1 Regular Language (RL), Regular Expression, Finite Automaton, and their equivalence
1.2 Context-free Language (CFL), Context-free Grammar, Pushdown Automaton, and their equivalence
1.3 Closure Properties of RL and CFL
1.4 Pumping Lemma of RL and CFL

Part 2: Computability Theory
2.1 Turing Machine
2.2 Church-Turing Thesis
2.3 Recursive (Decidable) and Recursively Enumerable (Semi-decidable) languages
2.4 The Halting Problem
2.5 Reduction

Part 3: Complexity Theory
3.1 Decision Problem
3.2 Certificate and Verification
3.3 P and NP
3.4 Polynomial-time Reduction
3.5 NPC and NP-hard
3.6 Approximation Algorithm (extra)

Remark:
- Contents are neat, since only the basics are introduced
- Contents are well-structured
- The three major proof techniques in this course, namely diagonalization, pumping lemma and reduction, are all based on contradiction/contrapositive

Personal comments:
The course could become much more abstract when going to part 2. But I think this course is still much more manageable than 3711 because you won't be required to know a large bunch of algorithms or write a very tedious proof (especially induction proof...) or dry-run on some large example (e.g. graph with at least 10 vertices).

This course does not quite focus on practical aspect, e.g. scalability, language design, paradigm, pattern, reusability, etc. Even though there is a part talking about time complexity, the distinction between efficient and inefficient is whether the algorithm runs in polynomial time or exponential time, but not whether it is O(n) or O(n^3). In other words, O(n^1000) would be considered efficient in this course.

However, this course focuses on more primitive, or you can say, philosophical things. What is the mathematical definition of regular expression (the thing that you might use to search strings in some specific form)? What does it mean for a device to compute something? Is there anything that a computer cannot compute? What does it mean for a problem to be hard? What problems are the hardest among hard problems?

This course will give you a completely different mindset to think about computer and a better appreciation of abstract maths. Indeed, I found this course to be beautiful (or lovely?) when all the nontrivial things/concepts are developed just based on the basic naive set theory.
comment_teaching:
The professor is certainly not the best presenter, but his approach of teaching is simple and intuitive. Attending the lectures did clear up some concepts for me.

The teaching of TA is also quite good. He is quite attentive to students' comments to improve his teaching.

BTW
At the start and at the end of semester, the professor raises some interesting comments about the course. One of them goes like this: "This course is kind of useless to your research or anything unless you really want to know the concepts. For PG students, they have to take this course anyway, but I would advise UG students to take other courses." (He later added, "I do not mean UG students cannot handle this course. Just that there are more interesting things out there to explore.")

This is my first time hearing a professor to actually discourage students from taking a course (well, in other courses you might hear something like "you should think whether you want to proceed" instead of "go to take other courses instead"). My opinion is that, if you love abstract thinking, or if you hate programming, or even better, if both, then go for this course.
comment_grading:
The grading scheme of this semester is rather different:

Attendance - 10 points (Mean 8.5/10)
Written homework x4 - 20 points (Mean ~90/100)
Part 1 presentation - 30 points (Mean ~85/100)
Part 2 presentation - 25 points (Mean ~89/100)
Part 3 presentation - 25 points (Mean ~89/100)
========================
Total - 110 points

The grading curve itself is quite nice. Full in attendance, almost full in homework, getting mean in all three presentations => A-

The course seems rather tempting, right? No exam, just present something, try to get a mean score, and then you have an A range.

But you should note that, a large proportion of students are PHD students who probably have more presentation experience (in thesis defense) than UG students, since this course is primarily for them. Also, judging from the Zoom meeting, many of them are NDS who are probably good at maths. So, don't treat the course too lightly.

The presentation scores are concentrated around the high 80s/90s. It is a bit competitive, but I think as long as you really comprehend the concept, generalize the details and give a big picture, and say something intelligible, then the TA should not be harsh in giving your grade. In other words, you don't need to have extraordinary presentation skills to obtain a high mark.

The professor seems to like this kind of grading scheme. So there is a chance that he retains this scheme even after the pandemic has gone.
comment_workload:
Attendance:
You need to be present in Zoom, but if you like, you can afk. I do advise you to pay attention, though.
(The professor would give out some answers for homework 3 and 4 at the end of two of the lessons, with the recording switched off.)

4 Homework:
Intermediate difficulty. If you don't know how to do, go google or find source (or listen to the professor's hints/answers).

3 Presentation:
You need to design your slide, write a script, and then do a recording. It is okay to just read aloud your script (better in a natural way, of course).

The workload should be quite manageable.

author: Jeff Sun
title: Theory of Presentation: Don't take it unless you are a CS RPG
comment_content:
The topics are indeed interesting.

See https://home.cse.ust.hk/~lzhang/teach/comp3721/ for details.

But this has been my nightmare of the semester, see the other sections.
comment_teaching:
Not very good.
He speaks monotonically, with heavy clothes, a hat, and an exaggeratingly large and thick mask, even if it was in the summer. I never saw his face. It feels like a robot paraphrasing the notes on the iPad and reading them out.
comment_grading:
Random grading under a strange scheme.

4 Homework Assignments: 4% x 4 = 16%
Part 1 video presentation:  30%
Part 2 video presentation:  24%
Part 3 video presentation:  20%
Class participation:  10%

This is a CS theory course, so I do not think this makes any sense. What kind of CS theory course counts attendance, and uses presentations as its major evaluation scheme. And, seriously, there are no exams, in this course?

The main point of the presentations is compressing everything he has taught in 15/12/10 minutes, and you are graded probably by how well the ppts are designed, whether you look good, or simply the mood of the grader. You don't need to think, just compress everything.

There is a similarity check for each presentation and homework, and harsh penalties apply. BUT, it never works! On the one hand, you may get high similarity scores for original works, due to the nature of the contents in the course, and, the assignments are never changed over the years. They rerun Turnitin after the DDL so that the similarity scores go up again, and that's why I messed up HW1. On the other hand, those who are really good at plagiarism have tricks to circumvent the similarity checks. They may "borrow" the presentation materials from those in the past offerings with good grades, and use their tricks to get high scores and low similarities at the same time.

The attendance policy is also crazy. You are regarded as present iff you log in to the zoom. This means that if you attend in person with focus, but somehow the zoom got logged off, you don't get the attendance mark. If you "pretend" to attend by logging in your zoom in your bed, you get the attendance mark.

Believe me, many guys who got good grades do not understand most part of the course. They just reverse engineer according to the ridiculous grading scheme. I would be kicking their asses had there been an exam.
comment_workload: Random. Mainly depends on how much you want to polish your presentation, but I really have no idea how to polish it. But it seems that it's important not to use the old-school plain-white ppt backgrounds, probably one of the reasons why I got below mean for all the presentations.

author: depressedcubic
title: Why the fuck you no release annotated notes
comment_content:
True Prerequisite: Above average (w.r.t cs major) math maturity and knowledge of COMP 2711 + basic knowledge of big O notation. The rest of the 3711 content and linear algebra is completely unnecessary for the course

Basically 4 things

- Regular Expression
    - Finite State Automaton
    - Nondeterministic Finite State Automaton
- Context Free Grammar
    - Pushdown automata
==== midterm ====
- Turing Machine
    - Decidability
- P, NP, NP-completeness
    - PSPACE, NPSPACE

The content is quite interesting for tcs snob like me but feels a bit sparse and underwhelming, I personally think a bit more depth would be preferred. For example, the context free grammar part could do more exploration into things like deterministic pushdown automata which is more quite theoretical and also practical for parsing.

The most interesting part is, of course, the turing machine and the NP stuff. The whole final is basically problem reduction of different flavour. It is indeed a very good foundation to have if you wish to try out theoretical cs for yourself, but is honestly a bit underwhelming.

For any regular CS student looking to take this course simply to fulfil requirement, I highly recommend you think carefully. As this courses requires quite a bit of math maturity normally only obtained by either self study or experience in actual math course (like math 2033), this might be harder than expected despite the straightforward content and assessments.
comment_teaching:
It seems nevin comes up with weird ways to try to boost attendance every semester, this semster he opts to not release his annotated notes which might be the less cringey thing so far (doesn't mean it is any less mean but i guess it is understandable)

Notes is decent, around 3/4 is probably inherited from very old offering and the later parts is his own notes. Lecture is slow but in detail, i personally slept on them a lot but i think they are not bad if you need them.

Tutorial Material also not bad
comment_grading:
Dk if this is a CS ppl thing but somehow their grading for proofs is extremely lenient. Honestly would prefer a more rigorous marking for a course that's supposed to be a introduction to more theoretical side of cs but i guess they need those pg who is half forced to take this course to pass or smth.

Grading component:
HW
Midterm
Final
(no weighting because i forgor and it was on his website that he promptly took down the millisecond the final is over)

Midterm:
High: 98.5
Low: 10
Mean: 57.11
LQ, Median and UQ: 41.5, 59 and 74.5

Final:
High: 96 (?)
Low: 0
Mean: 62.9
LQ, Median and UQ: 51, 65.5, 75.5

The course syllabus states >90 is required for A range which is fair given the straightforward exam but distribution wise seems a bit cruel. Let's see how's the letter grade when it is out.

Please be reminded that this is one of the three PhD "required" (required as in they will need to either have taken two of them in home uni or else have to do it here) course in UST (the other two being 3511 and 3711) so there could be quite a bit of PhD student competing with you.
comment_workload:
The ideal amount of workload for a 3xxx level comp course, not overwhelming yet enough to make you actually learn the course well.

The homework is straightforward but not trivial. Takes a bit of time to do but is a very nice practice.

Cheatsheet (for some reason two ONE-sided A4 which is weird) is allowed so no need memorize stuff for the exams

author: C.C
title: Great course but teaching can improve
comment_content:
This course gave me a new perspective to think and argue mathematically about computation and decision problems. Overall the content is very interesting and stimulating.

This first part of the course introduces several mathematical models (DFA, CFL, TM) to describe computation and investigates the computation power of each such model e.g. given a string w does there exist a DFA that can tell (yes or no) whether the length |w| is a prime number ? (indeed this question is asked in the mid term)

The important techniques here to show a model "cannot answer" a decision problem are the pumping lemmas (for DFA, CFL) and diagonalization principle (for TM).

The second part talks about computational complexity. There're lots of open questions here e.g. Is there a TM that given a graph, can tell (yes or no) if there's a clique of size k in polynomial time ?

As you can see due to many unknown answers, the best we can do is to compare and group problems together. We put
each decision problem into classes like P (solvable by TM in polynomial time), NP (solvable by NTM in polynomial time) and LSPACE, NLSPACE, PSPACE (space complexities) based on what we already know.

With the technique of reduction for each of those classes we have a set of "hardest problems" in the class e.g. for NP it's NPC. This way if someone can show a problem in NPC is in P, we'll immediately know all problems in NP is in P (a huge result !)
comment_teaching:
Personally: A

Nevin is helpful as he replies piazza questions fast and seriously. Also in exam you can ask him for clarification of question and he'll help you. I believe he is a good person.

Teaching: B-

I think teaching is a bit unfriendly to students new to the field e.g. it's mentioned in class that 2SAT is in P, yes he gave a polynomial time algorithm and dry ran it on some examples but no explanation at all as to why the algorithm works for general case. I mean hey it isn't too hard to at least comment a bit on the intuition of why it works right ?

Overall, I believe making definitions clear (so you don't have something "coming out of nowhere") and adding more quick intuitive explanations will making the learning experience much better.

Indeed in addition to lecture videos, I also watched almost the whole series : "MIT 18.404J Theory of Computation, Fall 2020" on Youtube to get better understanding and prof. Sipser (also the author of the reference book) teaches VERY WELL !

TAs: A

Tutorial provides good exercises and the slides are very good. However, sometimes TA may not read your answer carefully and mark you wrong, be patient and discuss with them, they'll fix it in the end.

Special applause to TA ZHOU Siyuan, I can see he treats his work seriously.

remark: final exam occured at 0830 and paper check on same day 1930
comment_grading:
4 Assignments: 5% x 4 = 20%
Midterm   30%
Final:       50%

This semester on random days (not announced) Nevin may ask bonus questions (not hard) in class, each accounts 0.5% of overall score. This's not mentioned in the marking scheme but he ends up asking 6 bonus questions accounting a total of 3% overall score.

However I still ended up skipping all classes (except the first). I think by teaching better or telling us exactly how many bonus questions will be asked (remove the randomness) could better encourage us to go to class in person.

Overall : ~89/100 (Est. UQ: 81, Est. High: 97.3)
Grade: A

Not particularly good or bad. Indeed I got 1 non-trivial question wrong in the mid term, it's about proving right-linear CFG = REGULAR. I tried to prove by induction, maybe due to unfamiliarity with CFG I got it wrong, very disappointed in myself :(
comment_workload:
Usual for UG course (although the majority of students are PhD)

4 Assignments are not hard and they help you understand the materials better.

Mid term, final (both closed-book with two one-sided A4 cheat sheets) are appropriate. While there're some non-trivial questions, they may also ask questions DIRECTLY from lecture note e.g. "Show that in any representation system, there're always some languages that don't have finite representations" in Mid term worth of 5 marks (I remember) and this's exactly on lec note 03 p.10

author: Unicorn1
comment_content:
CONTENT
Content has not been changed for a long time. The referenced book was published before 2000.
- Regular Expressions
- Finite Automata
- Pumping Theorem
- Context Free Language
- Pushdown Automata
- Turing Machines
- P and NP
comment_teaching: I like his teaching style. Explain it at a good speed. Easy to follow but not bored.
comment_grading: The grading is very good. I was around the upper quantile for the all homework and the final was sightly below the upper quantile. I got A as a result
comment_workload: The workload is very light. Homework is not difficult and video presentation is also light.

author: Andyyip
comment_content:
- Regular Expressions
- Finite Automata
- Pumping Theorem
- Context Free Language
- Pushdown Automata
- Turing Machines
- P and NP

Contents are not too difficult but you may get lost sometimes during lectures as Nevin goes through the notes rather quickly.
Don't expect the course to be like other COMP courses. The focus is on proving. The contents are mostly based on previously taught materials. Make sure to ask Nevin if you have any questions. He is nice and don't be afraid to ask.
comment_teaching: Basically reading the notes directly. But notes are clear and easy to understand. A lot of examples were given as well so should not be difficult to follow.
comment_grading: Very lenient grading. You will get a B+ even getting mean in the final.
comment_workload:
3 Homework
2 Group presentations

Homework is usually 4-5 questions and it takes around a day to complete.
Group presentation takes around 2 days to finish. If you have good teammates that shouldn't be a problem.

author: QI
title: The Teaching is boring but the content is interesting
comment_content:
Topics covered include set theory and countability, formal languages, finite automata and regular languages, pushdown automata and context-free languages, Turing machines, undecidability, P and NP, NP completeness.
(Directly copied from the course info.)
Actually it is quite interesting, with a lot of theoretical things involved in. It will make an awesome COMP elective if you do not like coding.
comment_teaching: The lectures are really boring since the prof. is just reading the notes. But the tutorials are actually quite interesting. TA will pop up some interesting question every tutorial (that I attended, at least).
comment_grading:
I don't remember it very clearly, I think it is about 30% midterm, 50% final and 20% homework.

It is not really hard to get good marks in the midterm and final since not many details are involved in the exams. So as long as you get your solution clear and correct, you will get full marks for that problem. By the way, the exams are all open book, so you do not need to memorize anything.

The grade is actually quite good. You do not need to get a super high score to get an A+.
comment_workload:
Well, the workload actually really depend on your maths basis. It is really a COMP view of math. So if you are good at math, this is going to be a piece of cake.
By the way, some additional information, there is over a half of the students in this course are actually PG student. That usually means they do not that care about their grades, but they are really good. So actually the workload is pretty okay.

author: dysania
comment_content: The content mainly comes from the referenced textbook, and Nevin has his own lecture handouts and notes.
comment_teaching: Nevin likes teaching by writing notes on a whiteboard. It would be great for those who can keep up with him, but would make others feel difficult to catch him.
comment_grading: The grading is very great. Nevin said he would try to get as many students to pass as possible since this course is more like a PG course, and he did exactly that.
comment_workload: The workload would be a little bit heavy if you want to master the course content as much as possible (since you may need to refer to textbooks or other resources). But if you just want to pass the course, it's not that difficult thanks to the nice grading.

author: Simpure`
comment_content: The content is really old, I guess it has not changed for more than ten years.
comment_teaching: Prof. Nevin Zhang does not like to follow the materials provided for students, he has another set of materials and always write everything on the whiteboard.
comment_grading: The grading lies heavily on the exams, the homework only takes a small portion. Nevin is quite nice on the grading stuff, he always tries to help every PG student pass the course.
comment_workload: The workload is a little bit heavy for a postgraduate student, but may be adequate for an undergraduate student.

author: agoti
title: "A-"-pumping theory of computation
comment_content:
Automata Theory (up to CFG) and Decidability. Then some brief P and NP problems.

90% of the course is probably common sense for people who played in programming competition, and the rest 10% if you are interested in computing theory by yourself anyway.

It's always the part that the course decides to skip through being the ones that I wanted to know. Always.

Safe to say I learned nothing new from this course.
comment_teaching: Never really paid that much attention, but as mentioned in some of his own(?) surveyed review of the course, he really makes mistakes in his written notes quite often, and that does make the teaching hard to follow at times.
comment_grading:
Zhang said that he gives everyone "at least an B+ unless they screwed up", so I give him at least a B symbolically. (otherwise I would've given this C or lower)

Never in my life have I looked at a written exam and thought "i wish i could be doing that as exam" so bad though.

Aside from the HWs that don't really weigh that much, the grading mostly depends on presentations on the lectures taught. But the grading seems to be at mean for like 80% of the students anyway, which means almost everyone gets an A-, probably a handful of B+, A, and A+.

Upon request the TA did give some response on how the presentations are graded, which besides whether "one covers everything taught in the lecture" (remark: The videos are like ~12 minutes long covering 7-8 lectures), it is graded based on:
- whether you "show knowledge of the content taught"
- whether you "can provide examples and explain them"

Doing the "coverage" part gives you mean, and doing the extra points allegedly gives you higher than mean. I did exactly that and I got lower than mean.

The TA also provided a high-mark student example (while stating that "most highest mark students don't want to show their work"), but the presentation literally reiterated the lecture notes (and recycled the examples, verbatim) with some colored slides, which the professor states as "good organization".

And from a discussion with the professor, he seems to really like this grading format and will not change it anytime soon.
comment_workload:
Undecidable. The amount of workload does not scale at all with the final grade. You will get below mean regardless.

It's more load on your mental health than an actual workload.

author: cats
title: Interesting course - would recommend for TCS people
comment_content:
The content has not changed in forever, just check other reviews.

Overall, I think the content is pretty good. You get an idea of computational models along with a feel of how powerful they are/are not. For me, the last part is the most interesting - complexity theory. You can rigorously get to understand basic concepts in complexity theory as well as try out a few reductions.

I have to point out an annoying thing here - Nevin really wants you to attend classes. His midterm explicitly mentioned to *use methods learnt in class*. These methods are quite tedious, so usually you would not use them. This was the case previously, and it continues to be the case - if you are taking this course, remember to memorize the algorithms in lecture notes.

However, given that he has stopped taking attendance/giving bonus for attending, and that the final did not do anything like that, I would not hold it against him.
comment_teaching:
Never attended lectures after first class. It seems he teaches ok.

One odd thing is that he himself recommends people to not take this course. I concur with that for people who have no interest in TCS/want to learn "practical" things. Besides that, people should take this course.

This time, there was no attendance as happened before. To my knowledge, he also did not give "free points" for answering questions during lecture, as happened previous semester - a good thing.

Oh, the TAs are quite good - the notes are clear and a good overview of the lecture.
comment_grading:
Assignments : 4 x 5% = 20%
Midterm : 30%
Final : 50%

I have attached final and MT stats. Grades are not out yet, but given that Nevin said that I will "likely" get A+, I reckon it's a very good grade course (since I would expect an A on this grade). At the start of the course, Nevin also said that last time he gave 30% A range but the department argued with him.

Overall, assessments are a good measure of how well you understand the contents and are a good mix of easy, medium, and moderately difficult questions. The grading is somewhat lenient, but that's because of the TAs. Nevin himself is kind of strict.
comment_workload: Normal-ish (on the higher end?) for a 3xxx cs course. As mentioned before, the assessments are a good mix of easy/medium/moderately hard questions. For me, I usually check lectures notes when the hw is released and then do the homeworks - takes about a day.

author: 黄汝平
comment_content: Including the main content of Computation theory, and it is really helpful to someone who want to look the cs in a mathematics  perspective. It offers deep insights into the underlying mathematical structures that govern algorithms and computational processes, enabling learners to appreciate the theoretical limits of computation and the efficiency of various algorithms. By studying this area, one can gain a clearer understanding of not only the capabilities of computer systems but also the inherent challenges and constraints that arise in computational tasks.
comment_teaching: The professor has a remarkable ability to break down complex concepts into simpler terms that are easy to grasp. With clarity and patience, he guides students through challenging material. His teaching style encourages questions and has some bonus for problems in class. In addition, every class was recorded for after class study.
comment_grading: Very nice. Assigment 10%, midterm 30% and final 60%. I think nobody wa failed.
comment_workload: Not very heavy for someone understanding the course. There are 4 assignments and most of the problems were shown in class.

author: Aelheim
title: If you love abstract thinking
comment_content:
Set theory
Countability and Uncountability
Languages (regular, context-free, recursive, recursively enumerable)
Associated Grammars
Automata (DFA, NFA, Pushdown Automata, Turing Machine)
Closure Properties (RL, CFG)
Pumping Theorem (RL, CFG, Recursive L)
Undecidable Problems
P vs NP

The content is theoretical and there is a good deal of discrete math.
comment_teaching:
The teacher will provide lecture notes on his website before the actual lecture, the lecture supports what was explained in the teaching material by providing examples and detailing proofs.
The best way to apprehend this course is to read the teaching materials concerning the lectures before them.
comment_grading:
Grading Scheme
4 Homework Assignments: 4% x 4 = 16%
Part 1 video presentation:  30%
Part 2 video presentation:  24%
Part 3 video presentation:  20%
Class participation:  10%

One of the Homework Assignments was cancelled and as of now, we don't know yet how it will affect the Grading Scheme.
comment_workload:
We had three homeworks of  medium difficulty, it took me one day to finish each of them. The homeworks allows students to directly apply the concepts learned in the course and understand them better.

Instead of a final, students are required to work in groups of 2 and make 3 video presentations to explain the contents of the course.
The course is divided into three parts:
Part 1 - lecture 1 to 13 : 15-minute video
Part 2 - lecture 14 to 20 : 12-minute video
Part 3 - lecture 21 to 23 : 10-minute video
This is challenging because the videos should cover all materials seen in the corresponding lecture and the video's target audience are new students about to study the material.

A similarity score is computed for each submission and it can sometimes be challenging to be under the threshold because your transcript for the videos essentially quotes the course contents.

I put a C Grade for the workload because while the difficulty is not that high:
- writing transcripts meeting the similarity score requirement
- creating the slides for the videos
- getting a record where you speak very quickly without stammering (you usually need several takes)
- editing the video
does take a good amount of time.

author: CW
title: Recommended for CS students
comment_content:
Content is very structured and taught coherently. You can feel that everything is connected and have a glimpse on how computer scientists and mathematics look at these computing problems.

The entire course is mathematical, even more abstract than COMP2711 and COMP3711. In 3711 you might need to come up with a O(n logn) algorithm for points. In 3721, polynomial time is fine :) O(n^100) is good enough.

Recommended for all CS students.

Content:
- Sets, Relations, and Functions
- Languages and Regular Expressions
- Countability and uncountability
- Deterministic Finite Automata
- Nondeterministic Finite Automata
- Regular languages
- Pushdown automata
- Context-free grammars
- Pumping Theorem for CFLs
- Turing Machine
- The Halting Problem.
- Undecidable Problems
- P and NP
- NP-Completeness
comment_teaching: Teaching is clear. The instructor upload all lecture videos online. Personally I went to almost all the lectures and found them quite useful. Simply looking at the note doesn't give me the required intuition and/or insight.
comment_grading:
4 Assignments: 5% x 4 = 20%
Midterm   30%
Final:       50%

Last assignment is for self practice and everyone automatically gets full marks. As a lot of PhD are taking this course (if they havn't take it before, it is required by the CSE department), you can expect high mean in both midterm and final. Problems in exmas are generally not *very* difficult. Sometimes problems in homework can be even more challenging.

Cannot say much about grading in general as I don't have many samples. I think 90+ in both midterm and final can get you an A+.
comment_workload:
Four assignments to be submitted in hard copy in class. The last assignment is waived for self practice. Each one took me one or two days.

Two single-sided A4 cheatsheets are allows in midterm/final. There are not much to memorize.

author: Aloha
title: 計算機嘅數學模型
comment_content:
Countability
regular expression (language)
finite automa
pushdown automa
context free grammar (language)
pumping theorem
turing machine
decidability
NP-completeness

呢個course嘅內容比較mathematical 比3711更加mathematical
讀過MATH2033嘅人應該會易上手啲
考試/功課會有啲題目叫你design regular expression/context free grammar/finite automa/turing machine去compute一啲language 呢part比較考IQ
亦都有啲係要你Prove嘢 如prove 某decision problem is decidable/NP complete 呢part感覺上直接啲
但final個mean比midterm低10幾分 睇嚟多數人都比較擅長玩IQ題

一開始由最simple嘅regular expression講起 然後就係finite automate (類似finite state machine嘅嘢) 跟住就加個stack落去提升computation power嘅pushdown automa 到最後就係擁有與real computer同等計算能力的turing machine. midterm打後嘅內容都係圍繞住turing machine.
comment_teaching:
prof把聲好悶 教得都好悶 自己睇好似過癮啲
no comment to the tutorial as I skipped all of it
comment_grading:
20% homework * 4
30% midterm *1
50% final exam *1
Mean應該 ~ B/B+
comment_workload:
總共4份功課 每份4題左右 題目難度中等 唔識可以google
考試open book, open note 題目難度大部份同tutorial homework相近 算係幾有良心嘅exam
有少量turtorial題目可供操練
workload總括而言唔大

author: Gao Yihang
comment_content:
The subject matter of this course is undoubtedly important, covering major areas of theoretical computer science such as automata, formal languages, computability, and complexity theory. The issue is not so much what is being taught, but rather how the material is presented.

One major concern is the format of the learning materials. Instead of providing well-structured lecture slides or clearly organized teaching notes, the course depends heavily on text-based PDFs and annotated handwritten materials. For a course built around abstract definitions, formal reasoning, and long proof-based arguments, this format raises the barrier to understanding. In practice, students often need to spend significant time decoding the presentation of the material before they can even begin learning the concepts themselves.

In addition, the course assumes a high level of comfort with mathematical abstraction and formal proof techniques. For students outside the Theoretical Computer Science track, moving from basic logical foundations to reductions, decidability arguments, and complexity classifications is far from straightforward. As a result, although the content is academically valuable, the overall learning experience can be quite difficult for a large portion of the class.
comment_teaching:
In terms of teaching, the course seems to place more emphasis on in-person participation than on making the material easier to learn. Some of the supplementary explanations, annotations, or classroom clarifications do not appear to be made available in a complete and systematic way. This puts students who cannot rely entirely on physical attendance at a disadvantage. The issue is especially serious for postgraduate students, many of whom are also managing research, TA duties, or project work alongside coursework.

Another problem is that the course delivery is heavily text-driven, with limited use of structured explanations or more intuitive teaching aids. Theory courses are already demanding by nature, and without a clear framework, visual organization, or step-by-step examples, students can easily get lost in technical details. As a result, a great deal of time is spent trying to interpret the material rather than actually learning it.

The teaching experience would likely improve a lot if the course were designed with greater attention to accessibility for students from different backgrounds, for example through clearer slides, better-organized content, and more guided explanations of difficult ideas.
comment_grading:
The assessment structure of this course feels highly demanding, with very little room for error. This is particularly stressful for postgraduate students who must obtain at least a B grade in order to satisfy program requirements. The exams, especially the midterm, appear to be very challenging, and the overall student performance suggests that the level of support provided during the course may not be well aligned with the rigor of the assessments.

This concern is even more important because, for some postgraduate students, the course is not just another elective or background requirement. It plays a meaningful role in their academic progression, including preparation for later stages such as PhD candidacy. Under these circumstances, maintaining a strict grading standard without corresponding instructional support can create an unfairly high-risk situation.

I am also aware of several postgraduate students who were unable to achieve the required grade. This suggests that the problem may not simply be a matter of individual effort, but rather a broader mismatch between the course design, the assessment expectations, and the academic backgrounds of the students taking it. For a course with such an important gatekeeping function, a more balanced grading approach and stronger academic support would be highly desirable.
comment_workload:
The workload of this course feels disproportionately heavy relative to its credit value and practical return, especially for postgraduate students. The burden comes not only from assignments, but also from the amount of time required just to process and understand the course materials. In other words, the difficulty lies not simply in how much work there is, but in how costly it is to even get to the point where meaningful learning can begin.

Although the homework may look manageable on paper, the abstract presentation of the course and the lack of sufficiently clear supporting materials make even straightforward tasks far more time-consuming than they should be. For students who are also expected to maintain research productivity, this course can take up a substantial amount of time that would otherwise go to reading papers, running experiments, or advancing thesis work.

What makes this even more frustrating is that the high time investment does not necessarily translate into better outcomes. Because the material is highly theoretical and the assessments are demanding, students may still struggle to achieve the required grade even after putting in significant effort. For students outside the theory area, this creates a combination of high stress and relatively low academic return.

author: zizi32
comment_content: The course is sooo difficult, and I think the content is sooo abstract. It is mainly about languages, including regular languages and context-free ones, and Turing machines. And a lot of things about proof not calculation or programming
comment_teaching: I did not attend the class.
comment_grading: The grade is fair.
comment_workload: The workload is not too much. This course only has assignments and exams. And no attendance is taken for this course. I never attend any class for this course. Honestly speaking, just reading slides is fairly enough for passing this course.

author: hao_chen
comment_content: Well, this course is interesting. The content of this course is very rich, you can learn a lot from this course. The content mainly comes from the referenced textbook, and Nevin has his own lecture handouts and notes.
comment_teaching: The teaching style of Nevin is interesting. Nevin likes teaching by writing notes on a whiteboard. It would be great for those who can keep up with him, but would make others feel difficult to catch him.
comment_grading: This is the best part. The grading is very great. Nevin said he would try to get as many students to pass as possible since this course is more like a PG course, and he did exactly that.
comment_workload: The workload would be a little bit heavy if you want to master the course content as much as possible (since you may need to refer to textbooks or other resources). But if you just want to pass the course, it's not that difficult thanks to the nice grading.

author: oopsray
comment_content: This is a good course.
comment_teaching: Overall, the teaching is quite good and clear. I just got clear after the attendance, which makes me more familiar with the ML techniques.
comment_grading: The grading is very reasonable and transparent. The criteria for assignments and exams are clear, so you always know what is expected of you. I felt that the scores accurately reflected my understanding of the material, and there were no unfair surprises in how we were evaluated.
comment_workload: The workload is appropriate, and the difficulty level of this course is well-distributed. The homework is manageable and helpful, as it reinforces the lecture material without being overwhelming or too time consuming. Overall, the pacing is just right, allowing enough time to really understand the concepts.

author: henryhc
title: Useful course
comment_content:
Includes regular language, context-free language, automata, set theory and complexity.
It feels like a mix of discrete math and compiler.
The content is useful if you are interested in theoretical computer science or you want to sign a programming language.
comment_teaching:
The professor make clear explanation on the concepts and so as the TA.
The course was delivered online and the situation may be different in this year.
comment_grading: The grading is friendly. I don't think I am a hardworking student but I got an A+.
comment_workload:
Four homeworks with median difficulty, I spent one day to finish each of them. They are useful because they will drive you to understand the course from a different prospective.
Also there are three presentations of 5 to 10 minutes. The content of the presentation are your understanding of the concepts learned in this course. I spent one or two days preparing for each of them.

There is no final exam.

author: pca
comment_content:
Standard stuff:
Computability theory: regular expression, context-free grammar, Turing machine, halting problem, ...
Complexity theory: polynomial time, NP, and NP-complete.

Grab a textbook, read the proofs, and you would be fine.

Would be better if LL/LR language and parser stuff can be included.
comment_teaching: The teaching is pretty good, the professor explained the ideas and proofs clearly and did have interaction with students.
comment_grading:
Another post provided very detailed statistics so I would not repeat them.

Homeworks: 98, 91, 100, 94.
Presentations: 96, 97, 98.
Grade: A+

Pretty good grade indeed. Recommended even if you suck at math like me.
comment_workload:
The 4 assignments are simple, require at most 2 hours for each.

3 presentations, students have to prepare the slides, write the transcript, and record the video. This can take a long time.

Tips for presentation: use beamer, they seem to like it. I've got a relatively good score even with my poor presentation skills...
(I spend 2 days for each presentation, as I draw every single diagram with LaTeX... as a bonus, my LaTeX skill for beamer is greatly improved)

author: blkwl
title: The exam is too difficult for PG students
comment_content:
While the curriculum covers foundational topics, including Finite State Automata, Context-Free Grammars, Turing Machines, and Complexity Classes (P, NP, PSPACE), the delivery way is significantly outdated.

- Lack of Accessible Materials: The instructor does not provide standard lecture slides. Instead, students are expected to learn from PDF text sheets and handwritten annotated notes. This format is inefficient for learning and makes it unnecessarily difficult to parse abstract theoretical concepts.

- Abstract Complexity: The content requires a high degree of math familiarity. For those not specializing in Theoretical Computer Science, the jump from basic logic to complex problem reductions and decidability proofs is steep and time-consuming.
comment_teaching: The teaching approach appears designed to force physical attendance rather than facilitate understanding. The decision to withhold certain notes or annotations adds an extra layer of difficulty for PG students who may have research or TA commitments. The reliance on a "text-heavy" approach rather than visual or modular slides makes the learning curve much flatter and more frustrating than it needs to be.
comment_grading:
The grading is particularly punishing for those required to achieve a "B" grade for PG graduation. The exams (especially the Mid-term) are difficult. The mean scores reflect a gap between the provided study materials and the level of rigor expected in the assessments.

I am personally aware of several PG classmates who failed to achieve the required grade. For a course that serves as a prerequisite for PhD candidacy, the lack of a grading scheme that acknowledges the different standing of PG students is a major concern.
comment_workload:
The workload is excessive, particularly for PG students. Between deciphering the text-only notes and completing the "straightforward but non-trivial" homework, this course consumes a disproportionate amount of time that could be spent on research.

In addition, the abstract nature of the problems means that "putting in the hours" doesn't always guarantee a passing grade, leading to high stress and low ROI for non-theory students.
